the great Khmer temples—Angkor Wat, Bayon, or Preah Vihear—and you’ll encounter the Naga at almost every  ...    The most striking examples are the Naga balustrades lining the bridges and causeways. These stone serpents usually have five, seven, or nine heads, fanned out like a cobra's hood. The Naga serves as a protective guardian, a symbolic bridge between the human realm and the divine temple. #### 2. Naga Railings

Staircases leading into temples are often flanked by Naga railings. This represents the serpent as a guardian of sacred thresholds—keeping away evil and guiding the faithful. #### 3. Cosmic Symbolism

In Angkorian cosmology, the temple represents Mount Meru, the center of the universe. The Naga, linked to water and the underworld, encircles and supports this sacred space—mirroring its role in holding up  ...    National Symbolism: Nagas appear on Angkor Wat
